content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

February 24, 1977


Honorable Erich Auerbach
Department 15
Los Angeles Municipal Court
110 North Grand Avenue
Los Angeles, CA 90012

Dear Judge Auerbach:

This letter is to confirm your telephone conversa-
tion of February 7, 1977, with Nan Emmerson of our Los Angeles
office, concerning disclosure requirements of the Political
Reform Act.

In connection with the preparation of your annual
Statement of Economic Interests pursuant to Government Code
Section 87203, you asked whether the following items are re-
portable:

1. Pension payments in excess of $250 received from
   the U.S. government.

2. Interest in a community oil lease, the value of
   which you do not know.

3. Burial plots valued in excess of $1,000.

4. Repayment of a $300 loan made to a friend.

5. Reimbursement and per diem exceeding $250 re-
   ceived as a member of the Executive Board of
   the California Conference of Judges.

With respect to your first question you were informed
that the Commission staff is presently advising filers that they
need not report pensions received from the federal government as
income.

In response to your second question, you were advised
that you should report your interest in the community oil lease

TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE ADMINISTRATION (916) 322-5660 • EXECUTIVE LEGAL 322-5901 • ENFORCEMENT CONFLICT OF INTEREST 322-6441

Honorable Erich Guerbach
Page Two
February 24, 1977

as an investment if you estimate its fair market value to exceed
$1,000.

With regard to your burial plots, you should report them
as interests in real property.

In response to your fourth question, you were informed
that you should report the repayment of the loan as income.

Concerning your fifth question, Ms. Emmerson advised
that the answer depended on whether or not the California Conference of Judges is a charitable or educational organization
under Internal Revenue Code Section 501(c)(3), or a government
agency. If the Conference is not a charitable or educational
organization or a government agency, you should report reimbursement and per diem received from it as income.

I hope the above information is of assistance. If
you have further questions, please contact me. As promised,
regulations issued and opinions adopted by the Commission to
date regarding the disclosure provisions of Government Code Section 84200, et seq. are enclosed.

Sincerely,

Ted Prim
Staff Counsel
Legal Division

TP:jo

Enclosures: Opinions 75-041, 75-055, 75-094-A, 75-094-B,
75-135, 75-145, 75-163.

Regulations §§ 18727, 18728, 18740.

sections.question: In connection with the preparation of your annual Statement of Economic Interests pursuant to Government Code Section 87203, you asked whether the following items are reportable: 1. Pension payments in excess of $250 received from the U.S. government. 2. Interest in a community oil lease, the value of which you do not know. 3. Burial plots valued in excess of $1,000. 4. Repayment of a $300 loan made to a friend. 5. Reimbursement and per diem exceeding $250 received as a member of the Executive Board of the California Conference of Judges.
sections.conclusion: With respect to your first question you were informed that the Commission staff is presently advising filers that they need not report pensions received from the federal government as income. In response to your second question, you were advised that you should report your interest in the community oil lease as an investment if you estimate its fair market value to exceed $1,000. With regard to your burial plots, you should report them as interests in real property. In response to your fourth question, you were informed that you should report the repayment of the loan as income. Concerning your fifth question, Ms. Emmerson advised that the answer depended on whether or not the California Conference of Judges is a charitable or educational organization under Internal Revenue Code Section 501(c)(3), or a government agency. If the Conference is not a charitable or educational organization or a government agency, you should report reimbursement and per diem received from it as income.
sections.question_synthetic: A California judge sought clarification on which items must be disclosed in his annual Statement of Economic Interests, specifically regarding federal pension payments, community oil lease interests, burial plots, loan repayments, and reimbursements from the California Conference of Judges.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Federal pensions need not be reported; oil leases should be reported if valued over $1,000; burial plots must be reported as real property interests; loan repayments should be reported as income; and Conference reimbursements must be reported unless the Conference qualifies as a charitable, educational, or government organization.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Well-structured advice letter with clear question and conclusion sections. Confirms prior telephone conversation. Document includes specific regulations and opinion references as enclosures.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: In connection with the preparation of your annual Statement of Economic Interests pursuant to Government Code Section 87203, you asked whether the following items are reportable: 1. Pension payments in excess of $250 received from the U.S. government. 2. Interest in a community oil lease, the value of which you do not know. 3. Burial plots valued in excess of $1,000. 4. Repayment of a $300 loan made to a friend. 5. Reimbursement and per diem exceeding $250 received as a member of the Executive Board of the California Conference of Judges.

CONCLUSION: With respect to your first question you were informed that the Commission staff is presently advising filers that they need not report pensions received from the federal government as income. In response to your second question, you were advised that you should report your interest in the community oil lease as an investment if you estimate its fair market value to exceed $1,000. With regard to your burial plots, you should report them as interests in real property. In response to your fourth question, you were informed that you should report the repayment of the loan as income. Concerning your fifth question, Ms. Emmerson advised that the answer depended on whether or not the California Conference of Judges is a charitable or educational organization under Internal Revenue Code Section 501(c)(3), or a government agency. If the Conference is not a charitable or educational organization or a government agency, you should report reimbursement and per diem received from it as income.

embedding.summary: FPPC Staff Counsel confirmed telephone advice to Judge Auerbach regarding the reportability of five categories of income and assets on his annual Statement of Economic Interests under the Political Reform Act. The letter clarifies which items must be disclosed based on their nature and value.
